result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Attacker
    participant B as Responder B
    participant S as Server

    I->>B: start responder with xB = B
    I->>B: Message 3 = encrypt((y, B), pkB)
    Note over B: beginAparam(B,B)
    B->>S: (B, B)
    S-->>B: sign((pkB, B), skS)
    B-->>I: Message 6 = encrypt((y, Nb, B), pkB)
    I->>B: Fake Message 7 = encrypt(y, pkB)
    Note over B: Check passes because code compares decrypted m3 with NY (= y)
    Note over B: endBparam(B,B) and endBfull(B,B,pkB,pkB,y,Nb)
    B-->>I: sencrypt(secretBNa, y)
    I->>I: Decrypt with known y to obtain secretBNa
result.explanation: The responder completes the protocol on its own, without any matching initiator run. In the trace, the attacker starts B as responder, chooses a nonce y, and sends Message 3 as encrypt((y,B), pkB). B then returns Message 6 containing its fresh Nb, but the final check is buggy: it accepts Message 7 if it decrypts to NY (the attacker-chosen first nonce) instead of Nb (the responder's challenge). Since the attacker already knows y, it can send encrypt(y, pkB), make B fire endBparam/endBfull, and recover secretBNa from sencrypt(secretBNa, y). This is exactly why secretBNa secrecy and the responder authentication queries fail.
result.patch_suggestion:
In processResponder, change the final test to verify the responder's fresh nonce Nb, not the received nonce NY. Minimal local patch:

replace
if nonce_to_bitstring(NY) = decrypt(m3, skB) then

with
if nonce_to_bitstring(Nb) = decrypt(m3, skxB) then

At minimum, replacing NY by Nb blocks the shown attack; using skxB also keeps the check consistent with the selected responder identity.
result.notes: The trace-based attack is explained by the NY/Nb mix-up. The hardcoded skB/skA uses in the role code also look inconsistent with the chosen identities, but they are not needed for the specific counterexample shown here.
